issue_title: Nanosecond time data being sent even if time_precision set to something else
issue_body:
In 2.7.1, if I have e.g.:

    data = [{'fields': {'value': 12629},
                 'time': '2015-07-27T12:55:48.165231Z',
                 'measurement': 'foo'}]
    client.write_points(data, time_precision='u')

Then the 'precision' field is correctly set, but the timestamp is sent as a nanosecond integer value, e.g. the above is sent with:

    POST /write?db=test&precision=u
    ...skipped other headers here....
    foo value=12629 1438001748165231104\n

This leads to it being silently rejected by InfluxDB (i.e. after the above, a `SELECT * FROM foo` returns 0 results), which is probably a bug on their side too (though they don't document very well what should happen in this case).

(apologies for the many tickets by the way, I'm in the process of switching to this module from a reasonably sized code base, so starting to use this module quite heavily :) )

The boxcar background subtraction introduces a bias for features that are closer than `smoothing_size` + feature diameter together. This is described in my paper on [arXiv](http://arxiv.org/abs/1607.08819), page 11, top paragraph.

In the crocker-grier paper, the boxcar size is defined as `2w+1`, in which w is one pixel larger than the feature radius. The same as our `diameter`. However, we use `2 * diameter + 1` as a default value for the boxcar subtraction. This is a clear difference with the CG algorithm.

I am not sure if this was a choice, or if this is just a programming bug. If we choose to change it, the bandpass function (over [here](https://github.com/soft-matter/trackpy/blob/master/trackpy/preprocessing.py#L59)), needs to be changed.

@nkeim do you know what the matlab code does exactly?

issue_title: change of deploy strategy for deis to use resource aware schedulers.
issue_body:
Right now on a new release, deis pushes the new containers and destroys the old ones. Which works fine with fleet. For resource aware scheduler like mesos or swarm. Once the cluster is packed with containers, we cannot do a new release as the cluster has no resources left to schedule new containers.

Solutions :
1. Do a rolling release of container by container. destroy one container of old release and start one container of new release.
2. Rolling release of 50% of the containers for that release which takes logN time.
Since we are rebasing from Fleet to Kubernetes, we need a version of deisctl that is more like Kubectl.

My understanding is that the following is all that is required to cause requests, traces (exceptions) and custom events (logs) to be sent to Application Insights:
applicationinsights.flask.ext.AppInsights(flask.Flask("foo"))

I'm not completely clear on whether I need to be manually calling .flush() or not - please advise.

Either way, I've tried:
app = flask.Flask("foo")
app_insights = applicationinsights.flask.ext.AppInsights(app)
app.logger.info("test")
app_insights.flush()

Requests are logged but no custom events appear in app insights. No errors.

issue_title: Expand Chain object tests to use all mainnet VM rules
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The tests in `tests/core/chain-object` are all using only the `SpuriousDragonVM`

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/48e94db187ed0b6166a6572f499ef624d5f987ce/tests/core/fixtures.py#L13

We really should be running these tests across all of the mainnet VMs.

### How can it be fixed

Change the tests so that they run against each set of VM rules.  This is probably best done by parametrizing the chain fixtures that are used.

* Version: 4.2.1

### What was wrong?

In the current implementation the response will be parsed on every `recv` from the socket https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/providers/ipc.py#L175

If the response is big it can become a bottleneck. E.g. in ETL tasks https://github.com/medvedev1088/ethereum-etl

### How can it be fixed?

Is it possible to check the last bytes of the response first to see if they are a valid JSON RPC terminating character such as },] and only if true try to parse JSON?

issue_title: mkprof command doesn't write empty tables
issue_body:
The `mkprof` subcommand of the `delphin` command is meant to replicate [art](http://sweaglesw.org/linguistics/libtsdb/art)'s behavior, but currently empty table are not written. The `--full` option could be used for copying non-empty profiles, but there's no way to write the empty tables like `art` does.

Proposal: add a `--skeleton` option for the current behavior (only `item` and `relations` are written), and the default behavior writes all tables define in Relations, even if empty.

For example, we should consciously think through how we handle `ctrl-c` / `SIGINT`.  At a minimum we should attempt to clear all lock files before exiting.

issue_title: conda's configuration context is not initialized in conda.exports
issue_body: root cause of https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-smithy/issues/762

issue_title: Strange formatting of message when package not found
issue_body:
I'm trying to install `rpy2` while offline.  It is available in `~/anaconda/pkgs` so I had hoped that would be sufficient to be able to install it.  But Conda can't find it and reports this message:

Strangely worded and formatted error message when package is not found:

```
Solving package specifications: .


PackageNotFoundError: Package not found: '' Package missing in current osx-64 channels: 
  - rpy2
```

What does the empty single quoted string indicate (after *Package not found:*)?

Why do we put the Python exception name in the text we display to the end user so we effectively duplicate the message "PackageNotFoundError: Package not found:"?

If being offline is the  problem (which it might be), why don't we indicate something in a nice way instead of with a stack trace?

What does Conda need to be able to install the package from the local version?

issue_title: Fix prefix data cache with rm_rf for conda-build on windows
issue_body:
As reported by @mingwandroid:

conda-build uses rd on Windows to remove the prefix before calling `rm_rf`, so we need conda to forget about the installed packages.

I'm getting the following error after I did `echo ". /home/michael/miniconda2/etc/profile.d/conda.sh" >> ~/.bashrc`  and commented out `export PATH="/home/michael/miniconda2/bin:$PATH"` in my .bashrc file:
```

michael@Pascal:~$ conda activate

CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'.
If your shell is Bash or a Bourne variant, enable conda for the current user with

    $ echo ". /home/michael/miniconda2/etc/profile.d/conda.sh" >> ~/.bashrc

or, for all users, enable conda with

    $ sudo ln -s /home/michael/miniconda2/etc/profile.d/conda.sh /etc/profile.d/conda.sh

The options above will permanently enable the 'conda' command, but they do NOT
put conda's base (root) environment on PATH.  To do so, run

    $ conda activate

in your terminal, or to put the base environment on PATH permanently, run

    $ echo "conda activate" >> ~/.bashrc

Previous to conda 4.4, the recommended way to activate conda was to modify PATH in
your ~/.bashrc file.  You should manually remove the line that looks like

    export PATH="/home/michael/miniconda2/bin:$PATH"

^^^ The above line should NO LONGER be in your ~/.bashrc file! ^^^
```

conda version is 4.5.9

Also, this might help: if I first do `source activate 'myenv_name"` then do `source deactivate`, and then try `conda activate "myenv_name"`, it works fine (the environment is activated).

issue_title: Cannot reinstall packages with conda - Anaconda 2
issue_body:
## Current Behavior I am using Anaconda2 and Python 2.7. I reinstalled it and I am adding packages through conda. In particular, I tried installing the R Kernel but it didn't work. Last week I did the same operation and it was working -- and the machine did not have changes in its configuration. I encounter the same problem with other packages. The error I get is an EncodingError. I tried running ```$ conda create -n py2 python=2 ``` and ```conda activate py2```but it did not help. I would like to keep on using Python 2.7 (because it is required for other reasons). Any help would be very much appreciated. ### Steps to Reproduce * Install Anaconda2 (https://repo.anaconda.com/archive/Anaconda2-5.2.0-Linux-x86_64.sh) * Ensure that I have ```/home/user/username/anaconda2/bin:``` in PATH * Execute ```conda install -c r r-irkernel ``` * I get as response: ``` EncodingError: A unicode encoding or decoding error has occurred. Python 2 is the interpreter under which conda is running in your base environment. Replacing your base environment with one having Python 3 may help resolve this issue. If you still have a need for Python 2 environments, consider using 'conda create' and 'conda activate'. For example: $ conda create -n py2 python=2 $ conda activate py2 Error details: UnicodeEncodeError('ascii', u'r-dbi\xa0', 5, 6, 'ordinal not in range(128)') ``` * I tried installing ```conda install -c r r-essentials ```and it worked, but then I get the same error with some other packages like ``` conda install -c r r-dbi ```, while ```conda install -c r r-rmariadb``` worked. ## Expected Behavior * The R kernel should be installed * A new notebook should be possible to be created using R ## Environment Information

issue_title: Condarc `pkgs_dirs` setting does not understand environment variables
issue_body:
Tested with Conda 4.3.11 on ubuntu 16.04 and centos 7

The existing `envs_dirs` setting will interpret environment variables (very useful feature).
This behavior does not seem to have carried over to the new `pkgs_dirs` option and instead, the environment variable is treated as a verbatim directory name.

A possible workaround is to use the `CONDA_PKGS_DIRS` environment variable.

Example `.condarc`:
```
envs_dirs:
  - $CONDA_WORKING_DIR/envs
pkgs_dirs:
  - $CONDA_WORKING_DIR/pkgs
```

Then:
```
$ export CONDA_WORKING_DIR=/home/tim/Anaconda
$ conda info

Current conda install:

               platform : linux-64
          conda version : 4.3.11
       conda is private : False
      conda-env version : 4.3.11
    conda-build version : not installed
         python version : 2.7.13.final.0
       requests version : 2.12.4
       root environment : /home/tim/miniconda2  (writable)
    default environment : /home/tim/miniconda2
       envs directories : /home/tim/Anaconda/envs
                          /home/tim/miniconda2/envs
                          /home/tim/.conda/envs
          package cache : $CONDA_WORKING_DIR/pkgs
           channel URLs : https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r/linux-64
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r/noarch
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch
            config file : /home/tim/.condarc
           offline mode : False
             user-agent : conda/4.3.11 requests/2.12.4 CPython/2.7.13 Linux/4.4.0-59-generic debian/stretch/sid glibc/2.23
                UID:GID : 1000:1000
```

Following up on #2046 and tracking #1772, #3484, #313, #705, and #1610. 

Currently conda search: 
  
  - lists ton of irrelevant versions (e.g. too old) 
  - lists all different build types 
  - doesn't given any package information
  - lists package for other python versions 
  - shows same results as conda search [package_name] --outdated

conda search should: 

  - filter out packages that depend on a particular version of python #705 
  - not show outdated package information #372
  - not show packages for all python versions #3484
  - not show older version than the currently installed one #3484
  - prioritize exact matches #1772 
  - provide package information #2046

issue_title: Add "build.d" analogous to "activate.d" to activate script to support compiler configurations
issue_body:
I'm putting this feature proposal here instead of in `conda build`, because I think it could be structured here generically as a support mechanism that both `conda build` and other build systems using conda could utilize.

The idea is to do two things:
1. Add `$PREFIX/etc/conda/build.d` which is shell scripts or batch files which are sourced on activation in package building/development mode.
2. Add `-b` option to `activate`, so `$ source activate -b mydevenv` would source both `activate.d` scripts and `build.d` scripts.

With this mechanism in place, `conda build` can fully remove its hardcoded knowledge about Python versions <-> compiler settings, and recipes designed to work with multiple compiler settings can be written generically instead of having to do a `source compiler_config.sh` as described in https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/911. Basically, `conda build` would no longer call the MSVC `vcvars.bat` file, it would instead use `source activate -b _build` instead of `source activate _build`, so that the compiler setup would happen in a `build.d` file.

To keep Anaconda working the same, the `python` package could add a file to `build.d` which replicates the current behaviour. It would be nicer in my opinion for there to be a different metapackage for this purpose, that depend on the installed `python`, so that it would be possible to have `python` installed for scripting use without polluting the build environment. That would seem to require changing Anaconda and establishing new conventions for python recipes that build C/C++ extensions.

issue_title: Failure to install: ImportError: No module named auxlib

issue_title: Enhancement - Allow creation of Ethernet Sub-Interfaces

issue_body: In the users guide, if multiple tests are in the same testbench file, you can wrap the tests with a "while test_suite loop". What about when the tests are down one level where scan_tests_from_file is needed. It doesn't appear that the tests inside of the test_suite loop are scanned. The --list output lists just one test named tb_top_entity.all. I expected the tests within the test_suite loop to be listed. Is this usage of test_suite in a lower file (with scan_tests_from_file) supported? I'm just wondering if I missed something. If you don't currently support this usage, I'll just move everything up to the top testbench entity.

Curator at the moment is sightly incompatible with the AWS hosted ElasticSearch Service when using the `optimize` command because AWS ES does not expose the `/_cluster/state/metadata` endpoint (returns 401), which curator uses to determine if an index is closed.

I understand that perhaps the solution is to ask AWS to expose the endpoint. I've made a thread about it [here](https://forums.aws.amazon.com/thread.jspa?threadID=217758).

On the curator side, though, perhaps it could warn when it can't determine if a index is closed instead of crashing with an exception. Presumably it would just fail to optimize anyway if it's closed?

issue_title: Cannot create KeyVault using ServicePrincipal auth
issue_body: It is not possible to use `az keyvault create ...` when you are logged in with a service principal today unless you use --no-self-perms, which blocks automation scenarios.  It's also problematic since we guide users to create service principals instead of using user auth.

issue_title: CommandGroup `expand` method not compatible with Autorest 3 generated SDKs
issue_body:
The `expand` method attempts to flatten a complex object by examining its properties and creating arguments for the individual properties. Autorest 3 changes model types to no longer have strongly-typed constructors, but rather to accept keyword arguments. This is not compatible with the technique currently used, which involved reflecting on the constructor signature.

Until this is fixed, expand is only compatible with Autorest 2-based SDKs.

cc/ @rohit-joy @lmazuel @troydai

issue_title: Feature request: a special "<text>" value for the hooks.yaml "files" field
issue_body:
Hi,

I had to implement this kind of "binary-files-filtering-out" today:
https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/pre_commit_hooks/utils.py#L10

So I made a quick overview of the existing checkers, and I found only 2 that are targeting **any** text file: [end-of-file-fixer](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/hooks.yaml#L76) and [trailing-whitespaces](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/hooks.yaml#L106). Both use a long list of known text files suffixes.

If ever you think this use case could come in handy, I'd suggest to provide a magic value like `<any text file>` to the `files` parameter in _hooks.yaml_, so that hooks developers don't have to re-implement this filtering each time.

You can freely re-use the code I mentioned above if it comes handy to you.

Also, sorry for the 3-issues-opened-in-a-row :) I got really excited by this tool and ideas for improvements just flowed in ! But they are really just humble features suggestions.

Cheers

This is a feature request.

Hooks that modify the contents can be dangerous and surprising. While some developers will appreciate the auto-fix behavior, others (like me) find it distracting and disconcerting. issue_title: supply a stamp file to peru, which can be touched if there are changes
issue_body:
When calling from `make`, you usually need to touch a fake build stamp file to indicate that the peru rule has run. Otherwise there's no way for make to know whether it's run before or not, and then every other rule that depends on it will get rerun every time. Not ideal for builds where a no-op make still takes a while or produces a bunch of output.

But using a build stamp file is kind of annoying too, because it means peru isn't checking your dependencies for (evil) changes. If you want that behavior, you need a way to run peru and have it actually *tell you* whether any dependency files were changed. issue_title: MultipartReader Instance Method (ReadNextSectionAsync) throws IOException: Unexpected end of Stream, Error.
Upgrading dotnet core from 1.0 to 1.1.1 gets stuck on old dependencies in Visual Studio
issue_body:
Hello, when we upgraded from 1.0 to 1.1.1, it failed because all the dependencies in visual studio were still 1.0.  When you clean or rebuild the solution, it still doesn't remove the /obj file.

I was investigating this, and it turned out Visual Studio kept the `obj\project.assets.json` in tact. So any time you rebuild it *reuses* the `project.assets.json` 

This actually made me spend so much time triaging this. Eventually I figured it out. My coworker had this issue as well. Others might have this issue. There are two issues here:

1. The tooling, Visual Studio, should clean "obj" directory after every rebuild/clean.
2. When we Update the "Target framework" in Project properties to `.NETCoreApp 1.1` it should regenerate the `project.assets.json`

I apologize if this isn't the correct place.

Program 'dotnet-runtime-2.0.6-win-x64.exe' failed to run: The application has failed to start because its side-by-side configuration is

issue_title: Attack on domain verification with BGP hijacking
issue_body:
As far as I know you validate the domain only by accessing the site via HTTP. [As explained in a talk on  BlackHat] (https://www.blackhat.com/us-15/briefings.html#breaking-https-with-bgp-hijacking) it's theoretically possible to get certificate for a domain you don't control with bad BGP routing.

Can Let's Encrypt somehow mitigate this attack?
I mean as of what [I've read] (https://letsencrypt.org/howitworks/technology/) on the website the nonce is at first signed, so this is secure as I think.
But what's the verification with a DNS record, which is - according to the site - also offered as a verification possibility? As far as DNSSEC is not used this is not secure.

CC: @ximaera

issue_title: running client on OS X fails with permission errors
issue_body:
It seems that there is some python code that tries to test if a port is open which does not work unless running under sudo. (This also means the integration tests always fail on OS X machines.)

I believe that is being done in order to see if the client can later open that port itself. A nice solution would be to just try to open the port and then handle the failures there, instead. This has the nice property of removing a possible race between the time of port-check and the time of port-allocation where another service could take the port.

issue_title: account.registerDevice method not found. Schema changed?
issue_body:
I tried to subscribe to push notifications:
```python
from telethon import TelegramClient
from telethon import utils
from telethon.tl.functions.account import RegisterDeviceRequest

api_id = 1234
api_hash = '1234567890abcdef'
client = TelegramClient('session_name', api_id, api_hash)
client.start()

device_request = RegisterDeviceRequest(token_type=4, token='1234', other_uids=[])
push_settings = client(device_request)
```

resulting

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "user.py", line 11, in <module>
    push_settings = client(device_request)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/telethon/telegram_bare_client.py", line 441, in __call__
    result = self._invoke(call_receive, *requests)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/telethon/telegram_bare_client.py", line 530, in _invoke
    raise next(x.rpc_error for x in requests if x.rpc_error)
telethon.errors.rpc_error_list.InputMethodInvalidError: (InputMethodInvalidError(...), 'The invoked method does not exist anymore or has never existed')
```

I think reason is [official schema](https://core.telegram.org/schema)
`account.registerDevice#446c712c token_type:int token:string device_model:string system_version:string app_version:string app_sandbox:Bool lang_code:string = Bool;`
is different from schema in project
`account.registerDevice#f75874d1 token_type:int token:string other_uids:Vector<int> = Bool;`

According to [commit in pyrogram](https://github.com/pyrogram/pyrogram/commit/ea35fa24801e6767c6fd37e4221abb5abafdbbc9) telegram changed schema again recently. But I cannot find it in official docs.

issue_title: Behaviour when both workflow's step and its tool define a default value for an input
issue_body:
In the case where both a workflow defines a default value and the tool in the step also defines one, cwltool picks the default value in the tool.

I haven't seen this behaviour been defined anywhere, and I would expect the values in the tool to be overridden since this is the only way I've seen to 'hardcode' values in workflows, but I don't know if that makes sense for everybody.

For example:
workflow.cwl
```
[...]
steps:
  step:
    run: tool.cwl
    in:
      parameter:
        default: 'workflow'
[...]
```

```
[...]
baseCommand: echo
inputs:
  parameter:
    type: string
    default: tool
```

This would print `tool`, I'd expect it to print `workflow`.

Is this something expected? I can produce conformity tests and tests for cwltool if necessary.
It would be great to support GeoJSON simplestyle (https://github.com/mapbox/simplestyle-spec), as it already happens in geojson.io and GitHub.

issue_title: Per-Request Alterations To the Queryset doesn't work as described
issue_body:
As per this Django ticket: https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/11629 the functionality described by Tastypie here: http://django-tastypie.readthedocs.org/en/latest/cookbook.html#per-request-alterations-to-the-queryset doesn't work.

In particular, although the code runs fine right now (Django 1.7, Django 1.8), the time being used isn't what's implied by the documentation (it's the time when the queryset was created, not when the request occurred). Also, it seems in Django 1.9, this code will actually fail: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/internals/deprecation/#deprecation-removed-in-1-9
(search "Passing callable arguments to querysets will no longer be possible").

issue_title: Leaky Rectifier activation upcasts to float64
issue_body:
Hi,

I noticed that the leaky rectifier activation upcasts the gradient of the weights in the layer. 
```
UserWarning: You are creating a TensorVariable with float64 dtype. You requested an action via the Theano flag warn_float64={ignore,warn,raise,pdb}.
  return theano.tensor.nnet.relu(x, self.leakiness)
```
I think that is related to #402 and that was closed on #661. So I just added this to the call:
```return theano.tensor.nnet.relu(x, theano.tensor.constant(self.leakiness))```

Should that be enough? Or should I also report it to Theano repo?

Thanks,
HM

issue_title: Loading of binary with lots of symbols is slow
issue_body:
I've tried to load a PE binary with 27000 symbol, and it took 90seconds. 
Did some profiling:
![perf](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/759365/19621382/d8771ff2-9890-11e6-998f-454cf72cce16.png)

It turned out that the code spends a lot of time in the `provide_symbol` function in `cle/loader.py`. Its because the lookup for the symbols is done by a a loop (O(n)), and since we look up n symbols we end up with (O(n^)) runtime.

```
for reloc in obj.relocs:
  if reloc.symbol and reloc.symbol.name == name:
    reloc.relocate(solist)
```

I've did a quick patch to build a map from the symbols, and with this the time to call `provide_symbol` reduces greatly:
![perf2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/759365/19621440/51d1ae2a-9892-11e6-8898-5e151a6ed58c.png)

I didn't sent a pull-request because the patch is just a proof-of-concept and not done correctly.

Could you fix this?

I pulled the docker file and the Image ID is `mystifying_banach` instead of the expected
`angr`. Would save a couple of minutes if the Image ID is renamed as `angr` again. :)

I have a rather simple function which I was unable to solve using angr. It successfully solved ~1000 functions of a very similar structure. The goal is to select the argument (rcx) such that the return value becomes 0.

I suspect that the generated CFG might be an issue, since at least one basic block is reported by angr to be shorter than expected.

The script below first solves the problem by forcing the execution path to go through the basic block at 0x189 and then to 0x33e, the return instruction.

Then it tries to explore to 0x33e via simgr, which does not yield a path with rax != -1 (the error result).

What's noteworthy is that the basic block for 0x189 is reported to end at 0x315, rather than 0x324 (what IDA says).

```
JChJg/H/SYnSTTHCSDHKTQnRSAnKSYPx/0kh0Uk5wQ+EDgAAAEjHRCRY/////+mgAQAASLhQD/g4
6hrYuEi5+OYTjEFgWSMx0kGJ0Em5cIx9b/aOGVRJusuWJUihNppkSbulv7P8m846b0i+2Mg0W4lE
lHBIv9YVdACjvM18SLvC3+nE6hVzFEm+Q9pWlRKWKHBJvzAz0erG0te4SbwvDimURAn59knHxf//
//9Ii2wkME0x5UiJ6kwh6kiD9f9MIeVICepIiVQkIEwPr3wkOEyJfCQYTA+vdCQ4TIl0JBBIi1Qk
KE2Jxkkp1kwp80iJXCQISItUJEhIi1wkGE2Jxkkp3kmDxgBMicNIKdNMicJMKfJIAdNMicJIKdpI
iRQkSItUJFBIi1wkSEgB+kgB2kgp+kiLfCRASAHySAH6SCnySIt0JDhMicdIKfdIKfpIi3QkMEwB
2kgB8kwp2kyLXCQoTAHSTAHaTCnSTItUJCBNicNNKdNMKdpMi1QkGE2Jw0kp00yJwkwp0kkB00yJ

SCnKSAHCSIlUJFhIi0QkWEiDxGBbXV9eQVxBXUFeQV/DkF8NAA==
''')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    with open('/tmp/sc', 'wb') as f:
        f.write(sc)

    proj = angr.Project('/tmp/sc', load_options = {
        'main_opts': {
            'backend': 'blob',
            'custom_arch': 'x86_64',
        },
    })

    print
    block = proj.factory.block(0x189)
    print 'I would expect that 0x{:x} == 0x324, the end of that basic block'.format(
            block.instruction_addrs[-1])

    print
    print 'Now lets find an input with return value 0'
    print
    print 'Version 1, works by forcing through the correct basic block'
    s = proj.factory.entry_state(addr=0)
    inp = s.se.BVS('input', 8*8)
    s.stack_push(0x41414141) # ret addr
    s.regs.rcx = inp
    sm = proj.factory.simgr(s)
    sm.explore(find=0x189)
    assert len(sm.found) == 1
    s = sm.found[0]
    sm = proj.factory.simgr(s)
    sm.explore(find=0x33e)
    assert len(sm.found) == 1
    st=sm.found[0]
    st.se.add(st.regs.rax==0)
    print '{:x}'.format(st.se.eval(inp))

    print
    print 'Version 2, should work but does not appear to'
    s = proj.factory.entry_state(addr=0)
    inp = s.se.BVS('input', 8*8)
    s.stack_push(0x41414141) # ret addr
    s.regs.rcx = inp

    sm = proj.factory.simgr(s)
    sm.explore(find=0x33e)
    while sm.active:
        sm.run(n=20)
    for st in sm.found:
        st.se.add(st.regs.rax==0)
        try:
            print '{:x}'.format(st.se.eval(inp))
            break
        except angr.errors.SimUnsatError:
            pass
```

issue_title: setup.py needs README.md, but it is not in the tar.gz package
issue_body:
The setup.py file depends on the presence of README.md, but that file is not included in the .tar.gz version of the package.

With v0.1.4, this problem now comes up because the tar.gz version is the only one available on PyPI, so pip picks it.

With v0.1.3, this problem existed as well, but did not come up when installing via `pip install click-spinner`, because a .whl version existed in addition on PyPI, and pip apparently prefers the .whl version over the .tar.gz version. I don't fully understand why this does not surface in the .whl version, but I suspect that pip does not execute the setup.py when installing a .whl archive.

issue_title: wave.wavefun does not return xs within proper support
issue_body:
As can be seen [here](http://wavelets.pybytes.com/wavelet/db2/), the values returned by wave.wavefun although correct in 𝜙 and ψ, have wrong values for `xs` as they are only valid for the scaling function, not the wavelet. As I see it, fixing this requires a breaking change in API

`phi, psi, xs_phi, xs_psi = wave.wavefun(...)`

and of course putting into place code for compactly supported wavelets, some sensible alternative for wavelets without compact support, and probably something for custom wavelets.

Happy to contribute with this or any alternative API change or addition that addresses this.

issue_title: Add configuration to turn off HiveContext, use plain SqlContext instead
issue_body: See title. User should be able to flip a configuration to use a plain SqlContext instead of a full HiveContext.  The context should have the same name (sqlContext) regardless of which kind of context you're using, so the switch would be transparent for the user.

issue_title: Provide decomposed RPM names in pulp_smash/constants.py
issue_body:
Currently the name, epoch, version, release, and arch are encoded in the pulp_smash.constants.RPM and pulp_smash.constants.RPM2 constants, but not easily accessible. Some tests hard code in this data as a work around:

See:
* https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/master/pulp_smash/tests/rpm/api_v2/test_content_applicability.py#L25
* https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/master/pulp_smash/tests/rpm/api_v2/test_updateinfo.py#L100

To avoid this in the future, it has been suggested to instead provide this in a dicitonary in pulp_smash/constants.py and then concatenate the contents to form the full RPM name so only one reference will have to be updated in the future.

For reference: [RPM naming convention](http://ftp.rpm.org/max-rpm/ch-rpm-file-format.html)

Roughly it would look like this:
```python
# file: pulp_smash/constants.py
from types import MappingProxyType

RPM_DATA = MappingProxyType({
    'name' : 'bear',
     'epoch' : None,
     'version': '4.1',
     'release' : 1,
     'arch' : 'noarch',
     'vendor' : None,
 })

RPM  = '{}-{}{}-{}-{}.rpm'.format(
        RPM_DATA['name'],
        RPM_DATA['epoch'] + '!' if RPM_DATA['epoch'] != None else '',
        RPM_DATA['version'],
        RPM_DATA['release'],
        RPM_DATA['arch'],
 )
```

"Reset to Default" button in Django Admin
issue_body:
### Feature Request

This is a feature request. In the *Django Admin*, on the *Constance config* page, for each value, we could have a "Reset to Default" button. 

Right now, one could reset a value to default by Copy & Paste from the *DEFAULT* to the *VALUE* field but this is error prone.

### Describe the problem
Documentation is missing information about being able to have sorted CONSTANCE_CONFIG_FIELDSETS. While ordering on fields is clear, ordering on fieldsets isn't (and might not seem to be supported?). It would help to present things in a meaningful order. 

### Steps to reproduce
Missing here: https://django-constance.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

### System configuration

* Django version:  2.0
* Python version: 3.6

issue_title: File-related assertions should check file time
issue_body: The file-related assertions (e.g., assertFileEqualsBaseline) should check that the result file is newer than the baseline. This will help to make sure the user doesn't accidentally transpose the two file names (resulting in the tester deleting the baseline file instead of the test result!).

> The new Tensorboard refresh button looks great, and will certainly be a bit more civilized than rapidly swapping the horizontal axis view to update the data. :)

issue_title: Python Certification Tests Don't Handle Extra Messages
issue_body:
When running the Python certification tests on Windows, the interface is open to every application/service that runs on Windows. So, the second it become available many different things try to use it. This is breaking the Python certification tests that assume a 'perfect' scenario of just the messages from the OpenThread stack.

Here is an example of an exception I am getting because the Windows stack sends a packet the Python code doesn't understand:

```
During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Users\admin\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python36\lib\threading.py", line 916, in _bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "C:\Users\admin\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python36\lib\threading.py", line 864, in run
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)
  File "C:\thread\scripts\sniffer.py", line 85, in _sniffer_main_loop
    msg = self._message_factory.create(io.BytesIO(data))
  File "C:\thread\scripts\message.py", line 376, in create
    ipv6_packet = self._lowpan_parser.parse(lowpan_payload, message_info)
  File "C:\thread\scripts\lowpan.py", line 1093, in parse
    return self._handle_iphc_header(data, message_info)
  File "C:\thread\scripts\lowpan.py", line 1073, in _handle_iphc_header
    return self._ipv6_packet_factory.parse(io.BytesIO(decompressed_data), message_info)
  File "C:\thread\scripts\ipv6.py", line 876, in parse
    upper_layer_protocol = self._parse_upper_layer_protocol(data, next_header, message_info)
  File "C:\thread\scripts\ipv6.py", line 866, in _parse_upper_layer_protocol
    return factory.parse(data, message_info)
  File "C:\thread\scripts\ipv6.py", line 1037, in parse
    factory = self._get_icmpv6_body_factory(header.type)
  File "C:\thread\scripts\ipv6.py", line 1032, in _get_icmpv6_body_factory
    raise RuntimeError("Could not find factory to parse ICMP body. Unsupported ICMP type: {}".format(_type))
RuntimeError: Could not find factory to parse ICMP body. Unsupported ICMP type: 133
```

This is preventing all Python certification tests from being run on Windows.

issue_title: get_optics fails on some JUNOS versions (12.3R2, 11.4)
issue_body:
### Description of Issue/Question

`get_optics` fails on some JUNOS versions,  including 12.3 and 11.4 (admittedly < 12.1 supported).  
This is due to a simple space inserted in the output for Infinite light levels.

A simple change to [L61](https://github.com/napalm-automation/napalm-base/blob/0.25.0/napalm_base/constants.py#L61) in constants.py to add a space fixes this issue in my environment.  However, I realize this may not be universally true.   

_Perhaps this should be a regex instead of list membership tests in [junos.py](https://github.com/napalm-automation/napalm-junos/blob/develop/napalm_junos/junos.py#L1880)?_

**Local workaround**
```diff
diff --git a/napalm_base/constants.py b/napalm_base/constants.py
index bcfbff9..cb328dc 100644
--- a/napalm_base/constants.py
+++ b/napalm_base/constants.py
@@ -58,7 +58,7 @@ TRACEROUTE_NULL_HOST_NAME = '*'
 TRACEROUTE_NULL_IP_ADDRESS = '*'
 TRACEROUTE_VRF = ''

-OPTICS_NULL_LEVEL = '-Inf'
+OPTICS_NULL_LEVEL = '- Inf'

 PING_SOURCE = ''
 PING_TTL = 255
```

issue_title: survey link for my research project_Open Deep Learning Models
issue_body:
I am a Master Student at Syracuse University working on a thesis focusing on sharing and using Deep Learning Models.

I am inviting you to participate in a research project examining people’s experiences sharing and re-using deep learning models and contributing to develop them on various platforms. We are interested in your reasons for sharing or using pre-trained deep learning models as well as perceived barriers or reasons to not share or use such models.

I invite you to take part in an online five-minute survey. The survey contains questions about your experience of sharing and re-using deep learning models, your motivations and hesitations to do this and the basic demographic information.

The individual results from the survey will be kept confidential, but the summary results will be shared with the community. We hope the results will be informative to those who share and re-use models and for the deep learning community more generally.
survey
Thank you very much in advance.This is the survey link:

https://syracuseuniversity.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_a99mwqom0lrPFzf

Ayse Dalgali

I built a squeeze net using tflearn. The problem is that the accuracy is not high even when training (acc: 0.88). The network used 'categorical_crossentropy' loss . Besides, it added random crop and  featurewise zero center to the input layer. Anyone knows about it?

```
    signal.signal(signal.SIGUSR1, self.on_signusr1)
AttributeError: module 'signal' has no attribute 'SIGUSR1'
```

Allow a conf file to be filtered based on stanza pattern and key pattern.   Should be a blacklist/whitelist type of thing where the pattern can be set to keep or discard based on the mode picked.

Instead of implementing complex and/or logic (combinations of filters via some kind of mini expression language or complex CLI syntax) keep it as simple as possible and allow multiple filters to be easily chained together:

    ksconf filter discard props.conf --stanzas='^source::.*\.sample' | ksconf filter keep - --keys '(EXTRACT|REPORT|FIELDALIAS)-.*' --output props-searchhead.conf

The above example would read `props.conf` discard any sample file (eventgen) props stanzas, keep only search-time prop keys, then write the output to a new `props-searchhead.conf` file.


Use cases include:

1. Filtering conf files for role-specific deployment (like shown above)
1. Quickly dumping a single stanza out of a config file.  (Like a conf-aware `grep`-like command)
1. Dropping silly (superfluous) settings in savedsearches.conf that just get in the way in many contexts (List of fields, dispatch app, alert mode, visualization name, ....)
1. For use as part of complex deployment/delivery chain.

issue_title: Using python C API with pybind11
issue_body:
We have some old code that worked directly with Python.h and the python C API. Lots of methods pass around PyObjects, which are probably pretty close to py::object in terms of implementation. If I attempt to bind a function which returns a PyObject, I get type errors since it doesn't know what PyObject is.
I can create a dummy class binding to stop these errors, but then python just treats the returned value as a generic object.

<example.PyObject object at 0x7f92b4157fb8>

I can't find enough information on how Python resolves objects into built-in types, so I'm not sure how under ordinary circumstances in the past our pybound code "knew" that the returned PyObject was a list or an int or whatever. I imagine I probably need to provide some additional python accessors to the PyObject dummy class so that python can figure out what the returned object is. Can anyone provide guidance? Is it possible that py::object just wraps the PyObject built-in and a reinterpret_cast would do it?

issue_title: Update custom type_caster doc with a more complete example
issue_body: I have recently come across strange errors (```XXX undetected error```) when calling overloaded methods that take as input objects that have custom type_caster instantiations. After some debugging, I found that a custom pybind11 ```type_caster<>::load(handle,bool)``` method must not only return a ```bool``` to denote its successful conversion, but must also throw a ```reference_cast_error()``` when the ```handle``` is not the expected type (to facilitate its use in pybind11's try/catch dispatch for overloaded methods). Also, the failed attempts to convert the ```handle``` likely raise a python error which, if left uncleared, will manifest in strange behavior elsewhere in the program. The [readthedocs](https://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/master/advanced/cast/custom.html) for custom type casters doesn't really cover these crucial requirements on the ```load``` method. It would have been useful for me to read about this in the documentation, so I figure it would probably be useful for someone else to read it too!

Hi all,

Given that PIP 10 breaks the pybind11 include file finding routine (issue #1174), I propose that we push out a v2.2.3 patch release soon. Are there any other urgent things that should be merged?

Thanks,
Wenzel

cc @jagerman

Per https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/should-pki-web-certificates-private-keys-be-simple-to-explain/32011,  the various terms involved in using Certbot can be very confusing to novice users. We should ensure that throughout our documentation, the first time a technical term is used on any given page, it (a) is linked to a definition (possibly Wikipedia), and (b) the abbreviation is given after it, if we use the abbreviation later on. For instance:

This form is not appropriate to run daily because each certificate will be
renewed every day, which will quickly run into a rate limit set by the
[certificate authority](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Certificate_authority) (CA).

As part of getting distros to package more of our plugins, we've agreed to stop releasing all of our packages in lockstep. This means that instead of requiring that all of our packages have the same version number, people are able to mix and match versions. The benefit of this is distros (and us) don't have to repackage everything anytime we change a single package.

To do this, we need to:

1. Modify each `setup.py` to depend on the oldest version of its Certbot dependencies that is needed to work. Some digging needs to be done to figure out what this version is.
2. Set up tests in Travis to ensure the minimum versions required stays accurate. This can be a mix and match between the test-everything branch depending on what makes the most sense.

issue_title: LetsEncrypt fails to parse an Apache config file
issue_body:
## I use Ubuntu 16.04


## I ran this command and it produced this output: 
letsencrypt --apache

The apache plugin is not working; there may be problems with your existing configuration.
The error was: PluginError(('There has been an error in parsing the file (%s): %s', u'/etc/apache2/sites-available/000-default.conf.save.save', u'Syntax error'),)

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5908006/15638298/439c1632-2651-11e6-822f-e25fed8fd899.png)

Looks like this. I waited overnight and it's still installing. I'm running Ubuntu 12.04 and using the webroot plugin and a command like this:

`./certbot-auto certonly --webroot -w /a/domains/certain.cn/public_html/ -d certain.cn -d www.certain.cn`

Am I doing anything wrong?

I'm using Active Directory LDAP with following attributes:
dn: `CN=Doe\, John,CN=Users,DC=mycompany,DC=com`
cn: `Doe, John`

My configuration is the following:
`c.LDAPAuthenticator.allowed_groups = ['CN={username},CN=Users,DC=mycompany,DC=com'`
`c.LDAPAuthenticator.lookup_dn_user_dn_attribute = 'cn'`

It seems the authentication fails since the comma in the cn is not escaped [which is required for AD](https://social.technet.microsoft.com/wiki/contents/articles/5312.active-directory-characters-to-escape.aspx) and therefore doesn't match the dn. I get the following reported in the log:
```
[D 2018-03-02 20:47:41.249 JupyterHub ldapauthenticator:278] Attempting to bind jdoe with CN=Doe, John,CN=Users,DC=mycompany,DC=com
[D 2018-03-02 20:47:41.272 JupyterHub ldapauthenticator:328] Status of user bind jdoe with CN=Doe, John,CN=Users,DC=mycompany,DC=com : False
    LDAPBindError: automatic bind not successful - invalidCredentials
[
```



My guess is the escape_userdn_if_needed() function needs to handle this case since the ldap3 escape_filter_chars does not escape the comma.

```
def escape_userdn_if_needed(self, userdn):
        if self.escape_userdn:
            return escape_filter_chars(userdn)
        else:
            return userdn
```

If there's a way around this problem by simply changing the configuration please let me know.

issue_title: Handle unlocks for old balance proofs
issue_body:
If a misbehaving node closes the channel with one of its partner's *old* balance proof, the available merkle root wont match, because the channel state only contains the latest merkle tree. In this case Raiden must recompute the corresponding tree from the WAL to call unlock.

Note: No tokens will be lost, the valid merkle tree can always be restored from the WAL.

issue_title: Unable to query db inside factory ('Iterator' object has no attribute '_sa_instance_state')
Bug: No data gets saved when a parameter returns (too many?) nans
issue_body:
The DataSet cannot match save ranges if an array consists of nans.


### Steps to reproduce

The following MWE reproduces the unwanted behaviour:

```
import qcodes as qc
from qcodes.instrument.parameter import MultiParameter
from qcodes.tests.instrument_mocks import DummyInstrument

# set up the instruments
mydac = DummyInstrument('dac', gates=['ch1', 'ch2'])
mydmm = DummyInstrument('dmm', gates=['v1', 'v2'])

# multiparameter designed to break everything

class CrazyParameter(MultiParameter):
    
    def __init__(self, name, instrument, names=('first', 'second'),
                 shapes=((), ())):
        
        super().__init__(name=name, names=names, shapes=shapes,
                         instrument=instrument)
        
    def get(self):
        return (13,)  # THIS IS THE OFFENSIVE LINE CREATING "WRONG" DATA

mydmm.add_parameter(name='crazy',
                    parameter_class=CrazyParameter)

station = qc.Station(mydac, mydmm)

loop = qc.Loop(mydac.ch1.sweep(0, 1, num=10), delay=0.1).each(mydmm.crazy)
dataset = loop.run()
```

### Expected behaviour
The dataset should get written to disk

### Actual behaviour
The save ranges can never be matched (see `gnuplot_format`'s `write` method), so the arrays are always skipped.

### System
**MAC OSX**

**master**
commit 3d7baef4b7c4e7b1ae815965490ef9dfe7adf2da 

**qcodes commit**

Currently there are still a bunch of examples around that uses the old toymodel.py and should be updated. At least 

* docs/index.rst
* Qcodes location-format example.ipynb
* Metadata.ipynb

These should be rewritten

issue_title: The core_algorithm inside TuRF does not parallelize
issue_body:
Hi - 

It looks like the core_algorithm inside TuRF does not parallelize. It's a simple matter of changing turf.py with a few edits, but I am unsure how to open a pull request properly to do these changes.

It seems to me that the default padding value for Pytorch is 0.0 as apparent here:

```
torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_packed_sequence(sequence, batch_first=False, padding_value=0.0, total_length=None)
```

But, the padding value of Pytorch's text is rather 1.0:

https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/7f0ed40229d680e64dc3fefbb39f2dccc255eb07/torchtext/data/field.py#L254

`self.pad_token` comes at the second place resulting in second in `itos` and hence padding value of 1.

I think having two different padding values is a bad idea? Even though I presume they won't really mix up together.

Would it be possible to have `to_pil_image` work for 2D arrays and convert them to an `L` PIL Images? 

Currently, it converts 3 dimensional arrays to RGB if they have 3 channels and to L if they have one channel. 

However, like in the code below,  `to_pil_image` complains if it is called on a 2D array. 

Would it be simpler if 2D arrays are assumed to be single channel images so that `to_pil_image` converts them to `L` PIL images ? Thanks.

```
ipdb> import torchvision.transforms.functional as FT

ipdb> FT.to_pil_image(_points[:,:,0]) 
*** IndexError: tuple index out of range

ipdb> FT.to_pil_image(_points[:,:,:1])
<PIL.Image.Image image mode=L size=640x480 at 0x7FD4947341D0>
```

Microsoft Graph [documentation](https://graph.microsoft.io/en-us/docs/authorization/auth_overview) says that AzureAD endpoints are used, and so it would make sense to use AzureAD Oauth2 backend.

My biggest doubt is in `SOCIAL_AUTH_AZUREAD_OAUTH2_RESOURCE` where I though the correct value would be `https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me`
Unfortunately, I always get this error: 

> The application named https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me was not found in the tenant named ****. 

I think this is related to the fact that graph is not considered a resource by Azure AD but the documentation is a little scarce.

For my needs, I configured a new backend:
   
        def user_data(self, access_token, *args, **kwargs):
            """Grab user profile information from microsoft."""
            response = self.get_json('https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me',
                                 headers={'Authorization': 'Bearer ' + access_token})
            return response   

I would like assistance to understand if I should fork my developments or if I'm doing something wrong about AzureAD backend.

### Describe your issue

ps: they'll start to deprecate the API in June 2018

There are several discussions here that are somewhat logically independent, but linked:

* Should we use the asyncio (or some other third party) event loop *internally* to implement our lowest-level IO primitives? This wouldn't necessarily change anything user visible; it would just outsource the dirty business of calling `epoll` and friends to someone else. Let's call this feature **io-via-asyncio**.

* Should it be possible to run trio on top of an asyncio event loop? e.g., `loop.run_until_complete(trio.run_in_asyncio, ...)`, to allow asyncio applications to call into trio code. Let's call this **trio-libs-on-asyncio**.

* How can we best allow asyncio libraries to be used from trio? Let's call this **asyncio-libs-on-trio**.

Some initial thoughts:

# io-via-asyncio

The major challenges here would be in coming up with a shim layer to implement trio's semantics in terms of the asyncio APIs, and extend those APIs where necessary. @1st1 has offered to add whatever APIs we need, which is great, but it isn't immediately obvious where to start.

In practice, it's very unlikely we could actually use the stdlib asyncio default event loop, because we'll definitely need at least some enhancements and bug fixes and the stdlib doesn't really get those on any kind of useful schedule. (Curio's experience with the `selectors` module has also made me wary of depending on the stdlib for this kind of thing.) So the assumption should be that we'd be using a third-party implementation like uvloop, or a hot-off-the-presses unstable version of asyncio ripped out of cpython master. (And this makes it faster to get bug fixes, but I think enhancements would still need to go on the PEP / CPython release timescale?)

uvloop doesn't play well with pypy (because of the cython), and inherits a number of limitations from libuv, e.g. no pluggable clock support, no cancellation support for most operations on windows (libuv source code does not contain the string `CancelIoEx`), and I don't see how to make `wait_all_tasks_blocked` work without some pretty extreme workarounds. If the pitch is "but this way you don't have to write your own I/O code!" then the part where I end up having to write a bunch of I/O code in C makes it somewhat less compelling :-(

And obviously the stdlib asyncio loops have similar limitations, or else uvloop couldn't be a drop-in replacement, in addition to the part where they aren't currently shipped in a usable form. And e.g. AFAICT from a quick look asyncio's IOCP cancellation is just broken (`_winapi.Overlapped.cancel` is synchronous, but `CancelIoEx` is asynchronous – see [e.g.](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/aa363789(v=vs.85).aspx) and search for "Wait for the I/O subsystem to acknowledge our cancellation"). Plus – and this is perhaps the most important issue – the current API is rather limited and using it would require overcoming some extreme abstraction skew. Implementing a fake socket object on top of a protocol/transport pair is going to involve a lot of complicated and relatively inefficient code, and then how do we implement `sendmsg`? Or raw or seqpacket or AF_BLUETOOTH sockets? trio supports all this stuff right now.

That's assuming we use the "protocol" APIs, which are the main ones and the only fully portable ones. We could also potentially restrict ourselves to just using `add_reader` and `add_writer`, and sticking to the `select` reactor on Windows (since the iocp reactor doesn't support these); that's actually enough to implement all the things we *really* support right now. But then we can't properly implement things like subprocess support (requires access to the kqueue object on kqueue platforms), or ever support iocp (see #52).

With sufficient effort, many of these limitations can be overcome or lived with. But it does look like some substantial effort. And it'll likely make things slower and more brittle architecturally.

Against this, the primary advantage would be that we don't have to maintain our own IO code. Given that IO code tends to be extremely tricky and have many obscure corner cases, this would be good. If we were using asyncio, then we could automatically take advantage of their bug fixes, and any work we put into testing and fixing bugs would automatically benefit all of asyncio's users.

This advantage isn't *urgent*, though, in the sense that what we have right now works, and (assuming the issues above are somehow fixed/worked around) we could switch what we do internally at any time.

And fundamentally, this isn't on trio's critical path: trio is an experiment, and the question we're trying to discover the answer to is whether trio's developer experience is so overwhelmingly better than traditional callback-based libraries that it can overcome their head start on ecosystems / maturity / familiarity. A 50% increase or reduction in the number of rare and obscure I/O bugs is not going to change the answer to this question.

So in the short/medium term this suggests that we should stick with our code, see how much trouble it causes, and continue to weigh that against the costs of switching. If we find ourselves wasting weeks trying to figure out why our [tcp stack is flaking out](https://glyph.twistedmatrix.com/2006/02/slaying-medusa.html) then that'd be a pretty good sign that we're on the wrong path. I honestly find it hard to predict; trio's code is written extremely carefully, and taking full advantage of every existing source I can get my hands on (by which I mean: blatantly stealing twisted's hard-won knowledge at every opportunity), but IO is hard.

The other advantage that this doesn't consider is that **io-via-asyncio** might help with the **trio-libs-on-asyncio** or **asyncio-libs-on-trio** features, so lets consider those.

# trio-libs-on-asyncio

This pretty much has **io-via-asyncio** as a minimal prerequisite, so see above. (We wouldn't necessarily have to switch to using only asyncio, but we would at least need to implement an asyncio backend, which is basically all the work.) In addition it would require some rearrangement of the `run` interface, which is not a big deal. And... there currently aren't any trio libs that people want to run on asyncio right now, so it doesn't seem super urgent :-). Perhaps it would attract people to trio if they thought that it was a good way to make libraries that work everywhere? But if that's your main motivation then even if we implemented this you would probably still be better off asyncio (or twisted or gevent) instead of trio.

The thing is, even if we made `trio.run` work as an asyncio coroutine, there still wouldn't be any sensible way for the asyncio and trio worlds to talk to each other. I guess it's fine if the *only* thing your library needs to expose is one-and-done functions that can be executed via `await trio.run_under_asyncio(...)` calls, but that leaves out a lot of use cases.

I'm not sure what a sensible communications channel would look like. Some sort of cross-world Queue object?

In general, it seems unlikely that using asyncio libraries on trio is never going to feel very natural, and ditto using trio libraries on asyncio is never going to feel very natural, because they have such different idiomatic ways of structuring code.

# asyncio-libs-on-trio

At least in the short term, this seems like the most interesting option. (And it might be *very* helpful in getting us over the early adoption hump!) What concerns me is how to let asyncio into the trio world in a controlled fashion that doesn't end up breaking all of trio's carefully created invariants. Maybe this is silly and we'd be better of just YOLOing it, worse-is-better style, but it makes me nervous. For example: if there's a global event loop full of callback spaghetti coexisting with trio's tidy task tree, then how do we do things like figure out when to exit? (Maybe 3.7's asyncio will be better in this regard; I know Yury is planning to propose a curio/trio-inspired `loop.run` in his asyncio updates PEP.)

One possible way to do this would be to *not* have a global loop object, but instead treat loops as something like nurseries: a specific place bound to a specific task. `with open_asyncio_loop() as loop: await loop.run_until_complete(...)`.

I have no idea how silly this would be. Technically what it would require is essentially implementing a custom asyncio event loop on top of trio's public API (so at least it has the advantage that it's something that doesn't require rearchitecting the entire trio core, and in fact could live in a separate library). I don't think we'd know how difficult this is until we try it – there's a bunch of stuff in the event loop interface, but most of it seems like it should map pretty straightforwardly onto relatively short trio code? And asyncio is designed to support the creation of new event loops by subclassing (sigh).

This seems like the most interesting place to experiment in the short term, though.

# note

These are very much "initial thoughts" as mentioned above; lets use this thread for further discussion.

issue_title: Running a search query vs requesting a specific object
issue_body:
I've seen areas of the GitLab API where a search query returns a subset of the properties and requesting the object by it's id/iid returns the full set. I'm looking at the `project-merge-request` function, run like this

> gitlab -o json -project-merge-request list --project myproj --iids 850

and noticed a difference in the response content with curl using [this](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/api/merge_requests.html#get-single-mr) API call. The documented API call is

> /api/v4/projects/myproj/merge_requests/850

Running in debug I see that python-gitlab is making this call

> /api/v4/projects/myproj/merge_requests?project_id=myproj&iids=850&all=False

Is there a different syntax to request the object directly?

issue_title: Windows: dependencies not filled in shared library manifests
issue_body:
We've been trying to add the ability to launch an IPython qt console from our C++ application. On Windows, things are failing because the import of (py)zmq fails (i.e. trying an "import zmq" in our python terminal fails). It turns out that this is because initthreads.pyd depends on the Visual Studio 2008 runtime (MSVCR90.DLL) but it does not know where to find it because the embedded manifest is lacking that information. So whereas in libzmq.dll the manifest contains the section:

`<dependency>
    <dependentAssembly>
      <assemblyIdentity type="win32" name="Microsoft.VC90.CRT" version="9.0.21022.8" processorArchitecture="amd64" publicKeyToken="1fc8b3b9a1e18e3b"></assemblyIdentity>
    </dependentAssembly>
</dependency>`

the corresponding section in initthreads.pyd is empty (as it is for the other pyzmq libraries - this is just the first to get loaded).

Would it be possible to fix the install kits to point to the runtime correctly?
I assume that the import works on running a regular python terminal because it already has the runtime loaded - our application is built with VS2010, but we still use the stock (VS2008) python underneath.
